coredata.dc:title: Sunlight-promoted photodegradation of Congo red by cadmium-sulfide decorated graphene aerogel
coredata.dc:creator: Kaushik, Jaidev
Himanshi,

Tripathi, Kumud Malika

coredata.dc:description: Herein, a simpler-viable methodology for the surface decoration of pear fruit derived graphene aerogel (GA) via cadmium sulfide (CdS) has been presented. GA can be easily synthesized from bio-mass, which provide an economic advantage. Surface decoration via CdS imparts photocatalytic activities in functionalized graphene aerogels (f-GA). CdS-f-GA is being explored here as a photocatalyst for the degradation of a toxic azo dye named Congo red in the presence of sunlight. The rate and mechanism associated with photodegradation were analyzed by performing kinetics and radical trap-based quenching experiments. Nuclear magnetic resonance and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses of the control and photodegraded products were performed to ensure the degradation of the organic framework of Congo red. Additionally, the real-life applicability of CdS-f-GA was also analyzed by degrading the dye in different types of industrial samples (via the method of external spiking), which can advance its practical relevance.

– radicals in presence of sunlight by CdS-f-GA in water; and (d) chemical reactions involved in the photodegradation of CR.

head.ce:title: Sunlight-promoted photodegradation of Congo red by cadmium-sulfide decorated graphene aerogel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a simpler-viable methodology for the surface decoration of pear fruit derived graphene aerogel (GA) via cadmium sulfide (CdS) has been presented. GA can be easily synthesized from bio-mass, which provide an economic advantage. Surface decoration via CdS imparts photocatalytic activities in functionalized graphene aerogels (f-GA). CdS-f-GA is being explored here as a photocatalyst for the degradation of a toxic azo dye named Congo red in the presence of sunlight. The rate and mechanism associated with photodegradation were analyzed by performing kinetics and radical trap-based quenching experiments. Nuclear magnetic resonance and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses of the control and photodegraded products were performed to ensure the degradation of the organic framework of Congo red. Additionally, the real-life applicability of CdS-f-GA was also analyzed by degrading the dye in different types of industrial samples (via the method of external spiking), which can advance its practical relevance.

ce:section.ce:para: Useable water sources, being the cellular need of all living entities, including some natural phenomenon, is in great danger because of contaminations from agricultural, sewage, and industrial waste(Alderete et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2021). Contamination by industrial waste, especially synthetic azo dyes(Almeida and Corso, 2014), has a strong and dangerous ability to deplete aquatic oxygen and interferes with photosynthetic processes, if discharged into water sources without proper treatment(Manivasakam, 2005; Puvaneswari et al., 2006; Hernández-Zamora et al., 2014). Additionally, another major threat of azo dye's pollution is its bioaccumulation, which can cause several health issues ranging from respiratory to neurological effects(Brown and De Vito, 1993; Lellis et al., 2019). Out of many azo dyes, Congo Red (CR), a salt consisting of benzidine, is well-known for its carcinogenic nature andits decomposition/elimination from waste effluents is bit tedious(Afkhami and Moosavi, 2010). Some well-known techniques used for removal of azo dyes are adsorption(Mezohegyi et al., 2007), flocculation(do Vale-Júnior et al., 2018), biodegradation(He et al., 2004), ultrafiltration(Zhang et al., 2013), etc. But at the same time, the generation of the secondary levels of pollutants is a serious matter of concen(Aggarwal et al., 2020). To avoid this, chemical oxidation and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are the commonly preferred techniques but have the cost-based limitations(Mohajerani et al., 2009).

Owing to these issues nanomaterial-based photocatalysis driven by renewable solar radiation is considered as one of the finest resolutions. The photocatalysts can efficiently harvest photons and generate charge carriers (electron and hole pairs)(Zhu et al., 2020; Bie et al., 2021) These electron-hole pairs then travel to the catalyst's surface, producing active reactive oxygen species (ROS) species that degrade the organic pollutants. Reports were in favor to use sunlight-promoted photocatalysis involving the degradation of organic dyes (Saini et al., 2021) and photo reduced the toxic higher oxidation states of metal ions to lower comparatively lesser harmful oxidation states.(Khare et al., 2018a; Anand et al., 2019; Bhati et al., 2019). For the same, nano-carbons, such as graphene-based materials, are investigated as excellent photocatalytic materials(Khare et al., 2018b; Singh et al., 2019).

In addition to graphene structures, these days, the 3-D architecture of graphene known as graphene aerogel (GA) is well explored(Worsley et al., 2010) because of its unique holographic characteristics and properties (Zhu et al., 2015; Gorgolis and Galiotis, 2017). GA has been commonly produced via many chemical routes, namely: template-directed reduction, chemical reduction cross-linking, hydrothermal, and carbonization(Gorgolis and Galiotis, 2017). Importantly, GA can also be synthesized from natural biomass sources such as winter melon(Li et al., 2014), watermelon(Dong et al., 2020), sugarcane(Li et al., 2015b) and the pear (Shukla et al., 2019) fruits which are viewed as an attractive renewable biomass precursors for the fabrication of 3D carbon nanostructures due to its high water content and textural characteristic (Shukla et al., 2019).

To make graphene-based photocatalysts, inclusions/introduction of some foreign materials in/over the base matrix of graphene is performed by the process of doping or surface decoration(An and Jimmy, 2011; Zhang et al., 2012; Low et al., 2016b). For this, Cadmium Sulfide (CdS) based materials have recently grabbed a lot of attention because of their excellent photocatalytic activity. They have a conduction band edge more negative than the reduction potential of protons, and having narrow bandgap(Hashem et al., 2021), and efficiently absorbs the visible light(Li et al., 2015a). However, recombination of electron-hole pairs has limited its applications, which can be overcome by composite fabrication(Alam et al., 2020). Considering the same, Li et al. have reported hydrogen production from water splitting through visible light by solvothermal prepared CdS-cluster over graphene nanosheets(Li et al., 2011). Similarly, reported hydrogen production under visible light irradiation by a CdS graphene oxide-based nanocomposite(Peng et al., 2012). Yu et al., have prepared reduced graphene oxide-CdS (rGO-CdS) composite by microwave and hydrothermal methods for the conversion of CO2 to solar fuel(Yu et al., 2014). In another report, Cao et al. performed tetracycline antibiotics degradation (∼83%), by CdS-carbon nanocomposite (Cao et al., 2019). Gao et al. have synthesized self-assembly assisted CdS-GO composite for the photodegradation of AO7 dye and also used as a disinfectant for Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis cells (Gao et al., 2013).

For the green and sustainable methodologies, we need to utilize renewable sources of energy and materials to think in environmental conservation, where we, in our route, should minimize the adverse effects, either in synthesis, application, or post-treatment procedures(Low et al., 2016a). Herein, we synthesized GA from the biomass (pear fruits) and these were functionalized with carboxylic groups for better solubilizations in water (named as f-GA) and then finally decorated with CdS nanoparticles to achieve (CdS-f-GA) via a simpler precipitation methodology performed at room temperature.

GA was synthesized using pear fruit by previously reported method (Myung et al., 2019). Nitric acid (HNO3) (used for the functionalization of GA), cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(CH3COOH)2·2H2O), dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent and sodium sulfide (Na2S) were of analytical grade and bought from Sigma-Aldrich, India. The Congo red (CR) dye [3,3'-(biphenyl-4,4′-diyldidiazene-2,1-diyl)bis(4-aminonaphthalene-1-sulfonate)] used as a pollutant mimic for the photodegradation was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. The industrial samples (named as S1, S2 and S3) were collected from the Sanganer textile industry, Jaipur, India on three different days.

CdS-f-GA was synthesized in two steps. At first, GA was synthesized as reported earlier(Myung et al., 2019), briefly, pear fruit is hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 24 h followed by pyrolysis at 1100 °C for 1 h. After that, the as-obtained hydrophobic GA was converted to its oxidized version as reported earlier(Kaushik et al., 2021a, 2021b). Then in the second step, decoration of the f-GA surface with CdS nanoparticles was performed by precipitation method to make it photoactive material. For which, 100 mg of f-GA was well dispersed in 40 mL of DMF solution by sonication for 30 min. After that, 120 mg of Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O was slowly added to this solution with vigorous stirring. With the continuation of stirring, 1 mL of 0.62 M of Na2S solution was added dropwise and the resulting solution was stirred for 10 min. Then, the mixture was centrifuged; the residue was collected followed by washing with water several times to remove unreacted ions. The final residue is dried at ∼80 °C and named as CdS-f-GA which further used for the sunlight-promoted photodegradation of CR.

Anionic diazo dye (CR) was chosen to check the photocatalytic efficiency of CdS-f-GA. Various control experiments were performed with a CR solution having a concentration of 40 mg L−1 by adding 0.4 mg mL−1 loading of CdS-f-GA in 100 mL conical flasks. After that, the solution was sonicated for 5 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium afterward kept under the illumination of sunlight for 120 min. Meanwhile, at different times aliquots of CR solution were collected for UV–vis quantitative analysis at a λmax of ∼501 nm. Kinetic studies were performed by making different concentrations of CR solutions (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100) mg L−1 keeping the constant catalyst loading (0.4 mg mL−1). Effect of loading was analyzed by varying catalyst loading from 0.2 to 0.8 mg mL−1 and keeping the concentration of CR (40 mg L−1) constant. Trap experiments were performed with a concentration of 20 mM of scavengers (isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BZQ), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA)), in which 40 mg L−1 of CR solution is prepared with 0.4 mg mL− 1 loading of CdS-f-GA. 1H NMR analysis of CR and degraded CR dye (after ∼150 min of photodegradation) was performed in deuterated dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) solvent as a reference. Photodegradation of industrial wastewater samples was checked with spiking 40 mg L−1 of CR in them externally and adding 0.4 mg mL− 1 loading of CdS-f-GA. Recycle studies were performed by using the centrifuged CdS-f-GA, washing with DI water repeatedly and subsequent drying at room temperature.

The Tukey test (one-way ANOVA) was performed via using Microsoft Excel software to see the significant changes in photodegradation rate. Statistical analysis on the basis of p-values was performed at the 5% significance level(Tripathi et al., 2017).

A FEI Tecnai G2 F30 was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), at an operating voltage of 300 kV. The comparative Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra and Raman spectra for f-GA and CdS-f-GA were achieved with an FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) and confocal Raman spectrometer (λex = 532 nm), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements for CdS-f-GA, are done on an ESCA+ Omicron Nanotechnology (Oxford Instruments) instrument. A Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer was used to obtain the UV–vis absorption spectra and a LABMAN-LMPH-10 pH meter was used to check the pH of solutions. Comparative 1H NMR spectra were obtained from JEOL ECS-400 (operating at 400 MHz) in the deuterated-DMSO solvent.

X-Ray Photoelectron Analysis: The elemental composition and surface-bonding of CdS-f-GA have been analyzed by the XPS analysis. Fig. 1 a represents the full survey scan spectra of CdS-f-GA showing the presence of various elemental binding energy peaks situated at ∼161.1 eV, ∼284.0 eV, ∼405.0 eV, and ∼532.0 eV, which are directly related to the elements S2p, C1s, Cd3d, and O1s, respectively. The elemental percentage analysis shows that the percentage of C1s, O1s, Cd3d and S2p are ∼75.8, ∼15.3, ∼4.4 and ∼4.5%, respectively as shown in Fig. 1b. An almost equal percentage of Cd3d and S2p was observed may be because of the formation of CdS stoichiometry. The elemental bondings were then further explored by deconvoluting the short scans. The deconvoluted spectra of C1s show peaks at ∼284.5 eV, ∼285.5 eV, and ∼287.9 eV corresponds to the CC, C–O and COO− bonds as shown in Fig. 1c. After that, deconvolution of O1s spectra (Fig. 1d) resulted in two peaks at ∼531.5 eV and ∼533.1 eV which could be related to the C–O and CO/COO− respectively. Further, the deconvoluted spectra of Cd3d show two peaks at ∼405.4 eV and ∼412.2 eV which are attributed to the Cd3d5/2 and Cd3d3/2, respectively as shown in Fig. 1e(Gao et al., 2013). The deconvoluted spectra of S2p shown in Fig. 1f show three peaks situated at ∼161.6 eV, ∼162.8 eV and ∼164.1 eV related to Cd–S2p3/2, Cd–S2p1/2 and C–S–C bonds, respectively(Tang et al., 2014). Thus, oxidative functionalization and the presence of CdS nanoparticles in the CdS-f-GA were supported by XPS analysis.

Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) and Raman Analysis: The presence of various chemical functionalities(Chai et al., 2019, 2020) and surface defects because of CdS decoration in f-GA were analyzed by FTIR and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. The comparative FTIR spectra of f-GA (black line) and CdS-f-GA (brown line) are shown in Fig. 2 a, which shows that f-GA exhibited six modes of vibrational stretching at ∼3420, (∼2883, ∼2818), ∼1727, ∼1601, ∼1380 and ∼1225 cm−1, representing the presence of –O-H, -C-H, COO-, CC, -O-H (bending) and C–O bonds. While in the case of CdS-f-GA these corresponding modes of vibrations are found to be shifted at ∼3434, (∼2922, ∼2855), ∼1731, ∼1592, ∼1370 and ∼1221 cm−1(Huong et al., 2020; Mohan et al., 2021). Further, the intensity of peaks in the composite also decreased dramatically, supporting the partial reduction of the oxygen-containing functional groups after the incorporation of CdS to f-GA(Senasu et al., 2018). The emergence of two new peaks at ∼777 and ∼667 cm−1 attributed to the formation of C–S–C and Cd–S bonds, respectively. A comparative Raman analysis has been performed to obtain the relative extent of graphitization and defects in f-GA and CdS-f-GA (Fig. 2b). Raman spectra of f-GA show two peaks at ∼1348 and ~1598 cm−1 related to the D (disordered carbon) and G (graphitic carbon) bands with ID/IG ratio of ∼2.51. After the decoration with CdS, D and G bands are shifted to ∼1339 and ∼1589 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, two new bands at ∼295 and ∼590 cm−1 have appeared which are characteristic of the presence of CdS nanoparticles(Peng et al., 2012). Both D and G bands are shifted to lower intensity may be because of the weakening of π-electron cloud after CdS composition and the ID/IG ratio was found to be ∼2.67(Kaveri et al., 2013).

The microstructure characterization of CdS-f-GA have been examined by TEM analysis. Fig. 3 a represents a low-resolution TEM image of f-GA which shows various graphene layers stacked with each other. Fig. 3b shows a low-resolution TEM image of CdS-f-GA, which demonstrates the presence of CdS nanoparticles on f-GA. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 3c shows nano-sized CdS nanoparticles in the form of spherical spots in CdS-f-GA. The corresponding zoomed image in Fig. 3d depicts the well-resolved fringes with an inter-planar d-spacing at different places (∼0.31 nm and ∼0.23 nm) corresponds to the different types of lattice fringes present in CdS nanoparticles decorated on f-GA(Cao et al., 2019).

The photocatalytic efficiency of CdS-f-GA was explored towards the degradation of CR in presence of sunlight. Initially various batch experiments as (i) control (blank) sample without adding any catalyst; (ii) CR with f-GA (in sunlight), (iii) CR with CdS (in sunlight), (iv) CR with CdS-f-GA (in dark), (v) CR with CdS-f-GA (in bulb-light), and (vi) CR with CdS-f-GA (in sunlight), were performed to check the comparative degardation ability of CdS-f-GA with control samples. The plot of relative change in concentration (C/C0) as a function of time for control and CdS-f-GA catalyst is shown in Fig. 4 a. The results showed that in presence of control and CdS-f-GA, CR is showing negligible effect in dark conditions and under the bulb-light. While in presence of sunlight, degradation of CR takes place in ∼120 min (p-value = 1.21458E-15) with CdS-f-GA only. Thus, CdS-f-GA has acted as an photocatalytic material that worked efficiently in the presence of sunlight. In contrast to our previous study, where f-GA were used as an adsorbent for the removal of cationic dyes(Kaushik et al., 2021b), incorporation of CdS turned it into a photocatalytic material,supporting the significant role of CdS over the matrix of f-GA. The comparative degradation efficiency of CdS-f-GA under the illumination of bulb-light and sunlight is separately plotted and shown in Fig. 4b, advocating the use of sunlight compared to the artificial light for the purpose of photodegradation.

To understand the plausible mechanism and rate of the photodegradation of CR by CdS-f-GA, different concentrations of CR dye were used keeping the constant dose of the catalyst. Fig. 4c shows a plot of C/C0 versus time to observe the influence of the catalyst on different concentrations of CR dye solutions. The experimental results indicate that the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst decreases with an increase in the concentration of CR. It can be explained that, as the initial concentration of dye increases, the requirement of the catalyst surface needed for complete degradation also increases. Also, if more CR molecules are present in the same solution, the possibility of photons going deep inside in the same illumination time will be difficult(Neppolian et al., 2002; Sakthivel et al., 2003). The photodegradation rate is calculated by fitting the experimental data into the pseudo-first-order kinetic model which can be expressed as Eq. (1) given below:

Where Kapp represents the apparent pseudo-first-order kinetic rate constant. C0 and C are the initial concentration and concentration at time t, respectively. The experimental data are well linear fitted to the pseudo-first-order kinetics with correlation coefficient values more than 0.98 as shown in Fig. 4d. The rate constant of CR degradation (40 mg L−1) is found to be 0.027 min−1 which was calculated from the slope of the linear fitted plot. The rate constant and corresponding half-life of different initial concentrations are summarized in the plot given in Fig. 4e, which shows that with an increase in concentration, the rate constant is decreasing and the half-life of degradation is increasing.(Bhati et al., 2019)

The effect of catalyst loading on the photo-degradation of CR was also investigated by varying the amount of photocatalyst and keeping the concentration of dye and other parameters constant (Fig. 4f). The bar plot indicates that % degradation first rises with catalyst loading and then drops significantly after 0.4 mg mL−1. The increase in the % degradation may attribute to an increase in the number of active sites as concentration rises. But, as it rises beyond a certain limit, CdS-f-GA do not absorb enough photons to generate active radical species in the solution to degrade CR.Therefore, the most effective photodegradation was observed at a certain fixed dosage (optimal loading 0.4 mg mL−1)(Sakthivel et al., 2003; Ioannidou et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic investigation (NMR and FTIR) of degraded product
ce:section.ce:para: The photodegraded products generated from the degradation of the complex organic framework of the dye in comparison to control CR have been studied by 1H NMR analysis(Bhati et al., 2018; Saini et al., 2019). Both the samples were prepared in deuterated DMSO solvent. Fig. 5 a represents the chemical structure of CR dye (all the protons are labeled from ‘A to H’) and Fig. 5b is showing the photographic images of vials filled with CR (left) and treated water (right) solution captured before and after photodegradation process, respectively. Fig. 5c shows the 1H spectra of CR (red line) which confirms the presence of various protons in CR dye in the aromatic region, while in the case of degraded dye (blue line) aromatic protons were absent, advocating the breakdown of CR structure. For better understanding, the aromatic and aliphatic regions are zoomed and plotted separately. The aromatic region of CR dye (Fig. 5d, red line) shows various δ shifts at ∼ d(8.76, 7.74), ∼d(8.45, 8.43), ∼s(8.31), ∼d(8.12, 8.10), ∼d(7.97, 7.95), ∼s(7.73), ∼t(7.59), and ∼t(7.50) which are related to the protons ‘A to H’ as labeled in the symmetric chemical structure of CR (Fig. 5a), (Pigorsch et al., 1994) while in case of degraded dye, no aromatic protons are observed (blue line). As well, the aliphatic region of CR shows the absence of aliphatic protons however for degraded dye, some proton signals at ∼4.71, ∼4.62, ∼2.24, ∼2.06, and ∼1.20 ppm can be seen (shown in Fig. 5e, blue line) which appeared because of breaking of the chemical structure of CR to aliphatic hydrocarbons of smaller molecular masses.

Additionally, a comparative FTIR analysis of CR and degraded CR also supported the NMR results favoring the photodegradation of CR (Fig. 5f). After the photodegradation of CR by CdS-f-GA, FTIR spectra of the degraded dye did not show the (-NN-) stretching associated to azo group at ∼1608 cm−1, ∼1444 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibrations at ∼1225 cm−1 (labeled in Fig. 5g as (i), (ii) and (iii)), which indicates that the complex structure of the dye has been broken(Chandanshive et al., 2017; Moon et al., 2018; Umamaheswari et al., 2018). Also, the appearance of some new peaks has emerged at ∼3202 cm−1, ∼988 cm−1, ∼619 cm−1 (shown in zoomed FTIR spectra in Fig. 5g) which could be related to the O–H stretching, CC bending, and other aliphatic hydrocarbon bending vibrations, respectively (formed as a result of photodegradation). As well based on the removal of CR using several photocatalytic and adsorbent materials including the present work are summarized in Table 1.

To find the identities of the active radical species which were possiblyresponsible for the photodegradation of CR dye, few trapping experiments were performed using different scavengers to capture the different reactive species (including the control set of the experiment where no scavengers were used)(Khare et al., 2018b). In general, during the photodegradation in an aqueous medium, photocatalyst generates three different types of radical species, which are hydroxyl radicals (OH˙), superoxide radicals (O2 •–) and a combination of electrons and holes (h+) and trapping reagents used to trap these radicals, are IPA, BZQ and Na2EDTA, respectively. Fig. 6 a is representing the plot of C/C0 vs time, which shows that the photodegradation process in the case of BZQ is significantly decreased as compared to the control sample which concludes that the superoxide (O2 •–) were mostly responsible for the photodegradation. While in the case of other scavengers photodegradation efficiency did not show much effect. Fig. 6b shows the rate of photodegradation of CR in presence of scavengers where the rate of photodegradation is minimum in case of BZQ (inset is the bar plot showing the lowest rate constant for BZQ scavenger). Based on the trap results, a schematic representation of the possible mechanism and pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of the dye is presented in Fig. 6c. Under the illumination of sunlight, the CdS electrons get promoted from the valance band (VB) to its conduction band (CB). After that the electrons are transferred/captured by the graphitic framework of f-GA (Li et al., 2011, 2015a; Bie et al., 2021) and leads to the promotion of electrons from HOMO to LUMO and finally generating the O2
– radicals. These O2
– individually or in the combination of with hydroxyl radicals, results in the photodegradation of dye into smaller hydrocarbons(Murcia et al., 2011) as shown in equations given in Fig. 6d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recyclability test and photodegradation of CR in industrial samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recyclability of the photocatalyst is also monitored by re-using the catalyst by washing with DI water several times and drying at room temperature and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. It can be clearly seen that CdS-f-GA is showing recyclability up to 5 cycles without losing its apparent performance. However, some decrease in the photodegradation efficiency is may be because of the loss of catalyst during the recyclability experiment. The photodegradation capacity of CdS-f-GA is also checked in industry liberated wastewater samples. For the same, an equal concentration (40 mg L−1) of CR dye was spiked in three industrial wastewater samples named as S1, S2 and S3 after that CdS-f-GA is added, to check the photodegradation in presence of sunlight. It can be seen from Fig. 7b that, in case of control samples (without CdS-f-GA) no degradation is observed. While in presence of CdS-f-GA, for samples S1 and S3, the photodegradation efficiency is found to be ∼73% and ∼97%, respectively. In the case of S2, degradation efficiency is only ∼40%, which may be because of either the precipitation of catalyst or the interference of other unknown impurities already present in the sample thus increasing the density of the solution and stopping the dispersion of photocatalyst and also retarding enough amount of sunlight to enter the CR solution.

In summary, CdS-f-GA photocatalyst was synthesized by a simple and facile approach via functionalization of GA followed by its decoration with CdS nanoparticles. The application of CdS-f-GA is explored for the degradation of CR in presence of sunlight. An absorption study was also performed to confirm the almost complete photodegradation of the dye in presence of sunlight as compared to the control sample. Photodegradation rate was studied by the kinetic models, and the mechanism governing the dye degradation via trap experiments used for trapping of radical species, confirms that superoxide radicals were majorly responsible for photodegradation. A comparative 1H NMR and FTIR analyses of CR and degraded products were also checked to confirm the breaking of the complex aromatic structure of the CR.For real-life analysis, CdS-f-GA performance is also checked in three different industrial wastewater samples by the method of external spiking. Based on the current presentation, the photocatalytic performance of CdS-f-GA could further be extended to the degradation of new generation pollutants such as pesticides, pharmaceutical waste, etc.

Jaidev Kaushik: Formal analysis, Investigation, Experimental work, Methodology, Writing-review & editing. Himanshi and Vishrant Kumar: Experimental work, Methodology, Writing-review & editing. Kumud Malika Tripathi & Sumit Kumar Sonkar: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.
